analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

operators: aten::mm gate-projection cohort (~511 nodes, sizes [[32,512],[512,2048],[32,2048]]) across 2 LSTM layers x ~128 timesteps x {input-to-hidden, hidden-to-hidden}
layer::unique::prologue (1 fused node containing the first unrolled timestep's gate SGEMMs)

bottleneck.description: Every gate projection in the unrolled LSTM dispatches to the SIMT FP32 scalar path. The dominant kernel name cutlass_80_simt_sgemm_128x32_8x5_tn_align1 encodes this directly: _simt_ means cuBLAS selected the scalar multiply-accumulate SIMT pipeline rather than Tensor Cores. smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active is 0.0 and tensor_core_active_pct is 0.0 on every one of the ~511 gate-GEMM launches, so every Blackwell Tensor Core is idle across ~95% of attributed trace time. The gate GEMM is [32,512] x [512,2048]: K=512 and N=2048 (4 gates x 512 hidden) are healthy, but M=32 (the batch) is tiny, so the SIMT kernel uses a 128x32 tile and reaches only ~8.2% achieved occupancy and ~16.4% sm_throughput. Because this dtype is FP32 the kernel cannot engage the Blackwell tensor pipeline at all. Promoting the GEMM operands to bf16 routes cuBLAS to a tensorop kernel (the cuDNN reference path in warnings already shows cutlass_80_tensorop_s1688gemm_* selected when bf16/TF32 tiles are available), engaging Tensor Cores. On Blackwell, bf16 Tensor Core peak is roughly an order of magnitude above SIMT FP32; for these K=512,N=2048 shapes the practical per-GEMM speedup is ~3-6x, bounded by the M=32 tile inefficiency (addressed further by OPT-2).

transformation.description: Promote the gate-projection GEMMs to bfloat16. The cleanest path for inference is model.bfloat16() before torch.compile so cuBLAS selects a cutlass tensorop GEMM. The aten-level FX pass inserts aten._to_copy casts to bfloat16 on both operands of every aten.mm.default and on the (mat1,mat2) of every aten.addmm.default, and casts the result back to float32 to preserve the recurrent-state dtype contract that downstream sigmoid/tanh/mul cell ops depend on. Inductor CSE folds the repeated weight casts (the two weight matrices W_ih and W_hh per layer are constant across all timesteps), so the per-timestep overhead is one activation cast, dwarfed by the Tensor Core GEMM speedup.
transformation.location: Aten-level FX graph (post-AOTAutograd decomposition): every aten.mm.default and aten.addmm.default node whose matmul operands are float32.
fx_steps: if node.op != 'call_function' or node.target not in TARGETS:
transformation.code_hint:
# Inference-only fast path (no FX surgery):
# model = model.eval().bfloat16()
# x = x.bfloat16()
# compiled = torch.compile(model, backend='inductor')
#
# Zero numerical-semantics-change alternative (FP32 accumulation, TF32 Tensor Cores):
# torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True
# torch.backends.cudnn.allow_tf32 = True   # before torch.compile
# This alone routes the SGEMM to cutlass_80_tensorop_s1688gemm_* (TF32) and is the
# lowest-risk first step if bf16 precision change is unacceptable.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Halves operand byte traffic for the gate GEMMs (FP32->BF16) and, more importantly, replaces the SIMT FP32 arithmetic pipeline with the bf16 Tensor Core MMA pipeline. Primary gain is compute throughput, not DRAM (these GEMMs are only ~26% DRAM and L2-resident at 71% hit rate). The lower-register tensorop kernel also lifts achieved_occupancy above the current ~8%.

optimizations.notes: Assumes inference-only (no autograd). The M=32 tile inefficiency limits per-GEMM Tensor Core utilization; OPT-2 raises M to seq*batch for the input projections and compounds with this pass. Estimate is a relative within-profile comparison; duration_ns is ncu-inflated. allow_tf32 is the conservative variant preserving FP32 accumulation.

operators: aten::mm gate-projection cohort: the input-to-hidden half (~255 nodes, one per timestep per layer) computing W_ih @ x_t with sizes [[32,512],[512,2048]]
layer::unique::prologue (input-projection SGEMM of the first timestep)

bottleneck.description: The unrolled LSTM issues two [32,512]x[512,2048] SGEMMs per timestep per layer: the input-to-hidden projection W_ih @ x_t and the hidden-to-hidden projection W_hh @ h_{t-1}. The input_op_ids chain (1015 -> 1017 -> 1019 -> 1021 ...) shows each timestep's gate matmul consuming the previous timestep's output, i.e. the graph is fully serialized into ~128 dependent launches per layer. But only the hidden-to-hidden term is genuinely recurrent; the input-to-hidden term W_ih @ x_t depends only on the input sequence and is independent across timesteps. Computing it one timestep at a time gives a degenerate M=32 GEMM that launches a 128-CTA grid ([8,2,8]) filling ~1 wave at ~8% occupancy and ~16% SM throughput, then immediately follows with a cublasLt splitKreduce_kernel (pure launch overhead for so small a problem). Batching all 128 timesteps of the input projection into a single [128*32, 512] x [512, 2048] = [4096,512]x[512,2048] GEMM raises M from 32 to 4096, turning ~128 tiny under-occupied launches per layer into one large well-tiled GEMM that saturates the SMs, and eliminates ~127 splitKreduce launches per layer.

transformation.description: Hoist the input-to-hidden gate projection out of the recurrence loop. At the functional (pre-AOTAutograd) IR level, the LSTM is most cleanly expressed with the full input sequence as a single node, so rewrite each layer to (1) compute Wx = F.linear(x_seq.reshape(seq*batch, in), W_ih, b_ih) once as a [seq*batch, 4*hidden] GEMM, reshape back to [seq, batch, 4*hidden], then (2) run the recurrence loop performing only the hidden-to-hidden F.linear(h_{t-1}, W_hh) and adding the precomputed Wx[t]. This is the standard 'precompute input projections' LSTM transform: it converts seq separate [batch,in]x[in,4h] GEMMs into one [seq*batch,in]x[in,4h] GEMM. The hidden-to-hidden term stays sequential because of the genuine data dependence on h_{t-1}. Best implemented at module-authoring time; the FX sketch below shows the node grouping when operating on the traced graph.
transformation.location: Dynamo FX graph (pre-AOTAutograd decomposition), or preferably the nn.Module source: the per-timestep input-projection F.linear calls sharing the same weight node and slicing the same input sequence.
fx_steps: #     def forward(self, x_seq, state):          # x_seq: [seq, batch, in]
#         Wx = Wx.view(seq, b, 4 * self.hidden)  # one big [seq*b, in]x[in,4h] GEMM
#             i, f, g, o = gates.chunk(4, dim=-1)
if node.op == 'call_function' and node.target is torch.nn.functional.linear:
# A weight reused across many timesteps whose activations are independent
# slices of one input tensor is the hoistable input projection; concatenate
# those activations, run one F.linear, then index the result per timestep.
transformation.code_hint:
# The single highest-leverage structural change is to stop relying on torch.compile
# unrolling the Python loop. If the model uses nn.LSTM, keep the cuDNN fused path
# (see OPT-3). If it must stay a hand-written cell loop, this input-projection
# hoist recovers most of the lost GEMM efficiency by raising M from 32 to seq*batch.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Reads each W_ih weight matrix once per layer instead of once per timestep, and eliminates ~127 splitKreduce launches per layer. Activation traffic is unchanged (same input bytes) but coalesced into one large GEMM with far better SM utilization.

optimizations.notes: Must be applied at functional level before AOTAutograd decomposition (after decomposition the shared-weight identity across timesteps is obscured by per-timestep aten.view/reshape nodes). Only the input-to-hidden projection is hoistable; the hidden-to-hidden recurrence is data-dependent and remains a per-timestep [32,512]x[512,2048] GEMM (still SIMT-bound, addressed by OPT-1). Combined with OPT-1 the hoisted [4096,512]x[512,2048] input GEMM is large enough to fully utilize Blackwell Tensor Cores. Numerically equivalent to the original LSTM.

operators: aten::mm gate-projection cohort (entire ~511-node unrolled recurrence) + per-op cublasLt::splitKreduce_kernel

bottleneck.description: torch.compile has unrolled the LSTM into ~1022 individual kernel launches (one SIMT SGEMM + one splitKreduce per gate projection, x2 GEMMs per cell x ~128 timesteps x 2 layers). Each launch processes a [32,512]x[512,2048] problem at ~8% occupancy, and CPU launch latency plus the splitKreduce postlude dominate per-step time. The warnings list (k_00009..k_00532) shows that the eager/reference path of this same model runs the cuDNN fused LSTM kernels elemWiseRNNcell + cutlass_80_tensorop_s1688gemm_64x64_32x6_tn_align4: cuDNN batches all four gates and uses persistent kernels across timesteps with a Tensor Core (s1688 = bf16/tf32 tensorop) GEMM, replacing the thousands of small SIMT launches with a handful of fused kernels. The Inductor decomposition discarded that fused path. Restoring it collapses the ~1022 launches and engages Tensor Cores natively.

transformation.description: Keep the LSTM on the cuDNN fused RNN path instead of letting Inductor unroll it. Two routes: (A, preferred) author the model with nn.LSTM (not a hand-written Python cell loop) and run the LSTM submodule eagerly under torch.compile so cuDNN's fused elemWiseRNNcell + tensorop GEMM kernels are used; the surrounding mean-pool and classifier still compile. (B) if a custom cell is required, replace the per-timestep elementwise gate computation with a single fused gate kernel and keep the four gate matmuls batched into one [.,4*hidden] GEMM (already implied by N=2048) so only one SGEMM+reduce pair runs per timestep rather than per gate. Route A directly substitutes the unrolled aten.mm subgraph with the cuDNN RNN op the profiler already shows is available on this device. NOTE: the match_target enum value F.scaled_dot_product_attention does not apply literally to an LSTM; it is the closest schema enum and the true target is the nn.LSTM / aten.lstm op site.
transformation.location: Functional level: the aten::lstm / nn.LSTM call site, before Dynamo decomposes it into the per-timestep aten.mm loop.
fx_steps: # Route A: prevent decomposition/unroll of the fused RNN op.
# Run the recurrent module eagerly so cuDNN fused kernels are used, while the
# compiled_head = torch.compile(self.head)  # compile only the head
#     y, _ = self.lstm(x)          # eager cuDNN fused path
# Route B (custom cell): keep the four gate GEMMs batched into one [.,4h] linear
# (already the case here: N=2048=4*512) and replace the separate
# sigmoid/tanh/mul elementwise nodes with a single fused pointwise kernel so
# Inductor emits one triton_poi_fused_* per timestep instead of several.
transformation.code_hint:
# Verify the fused path is taken: nsys should show elemWiseRNNcell + a small
# number of cutlass_80_tensorop_s1688gemm_* kernels for the LSTM region instead
# of hundreds of cutlass_80_simt_sgemm_128x32 + splitKreduce pairs.
# torch.backends.cudnn.enabled must hold True (it does on this capture).

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: cuDNN fused RNN keeps gate activations and cell state in registers/SMEM across the elementwise update, eliminating the round-trip writes of the four gate slices to HBM that the unrolled aten path performs each timestep, and removes ~511 splitKreduce launches entirely.

optimizations.notes: Structurally the largest win but it changes the execution backend for the recurrent region rather than transforming kernels in place, so it is MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with OPT-1/OPT-2 over the same recurrent matmuls (apply Route A OR (OPT-1+OPT-2), not both to the same nodes). Confidence is medium because exact cuDNN kernel selection and Blackwell s1688 tensorop availability depend on the runtime cuDNN version; the reference path in warnings confirms it is available on this device.

operators: All aten::mm / aten::addmm weight parameter nodes (W_ih, W_hh per layer; classifier weight) feeding the ~511 gate GEMMs

bottleneck.description: Two compile-time-decidable costs ride on every gate GEMM. First, each [32,512]x[512,2048] SGEMM is followed by a cublasLt splitKreduce_kernel (grid [64,2,1]): cuBLAS split the K=512 dimension across CTAs and needs a reduction epilogue, which for M=32 is pure overhead -- ~511 such reduce launches across the trace. max_autotune lets Inductor benchmark a non-split-K tile (or a Triton GEMM) that produces the result in one kernel. Second, weights are not frozen, so the _tn_ transposed-normal layout cuBLAS wants (encoded in the _tn_ suffix) is materialized per call and weight guards are re-checked each forward. freezing hoists the transpose and constant-folds the weights. These are incremental gains on top of OPT-1/OPT-2 and are zero-risk for inference.

transformation.description: Enable freezing and max_autotune so Inductor treats the LSTM weight parameters as compile-time constants (folding the _tn_ transpose and dropping per-call weight guards) and autotunes a GEMM configuration that avoids the split-K reduction epilogue for these small-M shapes. With OPT-1's bf16 weights in place, freezing additionally lets Inductor emit the pre-packed tensorop weight layout for maximum Tensor Core tile reuse.
transformation.location: torch.compile options dict / torch._inductor.config, applied during Inductor lowering.
transformation.code_hint:
# Requires model.eval(); freezing is incompatible with training.
# First call triggers autotune benchmarking (slow); cache persists in-process.
# torch._inductor.config.autotune_local_cache = True  # persist across runs.
# For an unrolled recurrence with hundreds of identical-shape GEMMs, the autotune
# result is found once and reused for every timestep, so the one-time cost amortizes.

estimated_impact.memory_traffic_reduction: Eliminates the per-GEMM split-K reduction epilogue kernels and the runtime weight transpose/guard work. After OPT-1, enables the pre-packed bf16 tensorop weight layout.

optimizations.notes: OPT-1 is listed as a prerequisite by pipeline level ordering (functional -> aten -> inductor_config), so bf16 weights are already in place when freezing emits the pre-packed layout. The splitKreduce elimination depends on the autotuner finding a non-split-K tile competitive for M=32; impact is workload-specific. Incompatible with torch.compile(dynamic=True) if seq_len or batch vary across calls. If OPT-3 Route A is adopted (cuDNN fused LSTM), this pass applies only to the classifier head.

global_notes: 1 operator below the 1% threshold omitted (combined ~0.01% of attributed time): aten::addmm classifier [[32,512],[512,10]] (0.014%, 6528 ns, 2 kernels). It is retained as a line item in the time_budget for completeness because it is the only non-gate-GEMM operator, but it is not reasoned about as a bottleneck.

hardware-limits.md not found on disk. Device NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (Sm100 / GB202 class) properties inferred from published Blackwell architecture: ~188 SMs at 128 FP32 CUDA cores per SM. The gate GEMM grid is [8,2,8]=128 CTAs, i.e. <1 wave, confirming severe wave underutilization at M=32 independent of the exact SM count; wave/occupancy conclusions are robust to a +/-20% SM-count error.

warp_cycles_per_instruction is null for all operators (counter removed on Blackwell Sm100). Latency-bound proxy applied: eligible_cycles_pct < ~20-30% indicates latency/launch-bound. The gate GEMMs report eligible_cycles_pct ~25% with achieved_occupancy ~8.2% and dram_throughput ~26% (not memory-bound) and tensor_core_active 0% -- the regime is small-M SIMT GEMM that cannot fill the machine, dominated by per-launch overhead and scalar FP32 arithmetic.

fused_kernel_double_count flag active: layer::unique::prologue is_fused=true and encapsulates the first unrolled timestep plus transpose/clone/zeros/unbind/mean setup. Its constituent gate-SGEMM kernels are reported once inside the prologue line item and are NOT double-counted against the granular aten::mm cohort, which begins at op_id=1017 (the second timestep onward).

The aten::mm gate-projection cohort is the unrolled stacked LSTM: input_op_ids form a strict chain (...1015 -> 1017 -> 1019 -> 1021...), each timestep consuming the prior timestep's hidden state. Sizes are uniformly [[32,512],[512,2048],[32,2048]] = (batch=32, hidden=512, 4 gates x 512 = 2048). Two GEMMs per cell (input-to-hidden, hidden-to-hidden) x ~128 timesteps x 2 layers => ~511 gate GEMMs, each paired with a cublasLt splitKreduce_kernel (~1022 attributed kernels total in the cohort). Per-GEMM aggregated total_duration_ns ~8384 ns is tightly clustered, so dynamic_shapes is NOT flagged.

The eager/reference cuDNN path is present in warnings[] (k_00000..k_00537) as 'flagged as initialization kernel' -- void elemWiseRNNcell<...cudnnRNNMode...> and cutlass_80_tensorop_s1688gemm_64x64_32x6_tn_align4. These are warm-up/uncompiled fused-LSTM kernels, correctly excluded from attribution (unattributed_kernels is empty). Their presence is direct evidence for OPT-3: a Tensor Core fused RNN path exists on this device, which the Inductor decomposition discarded.

All kernels run on stream_id=7 (no multi_stream_overlap). No cuda_graph_replay (compile_mode=inductor). No clock_domain anomaly (start_ns ~8.1e9, positive, < 1e15). high_unattributed not flagged (unattributed_kernels is empty).

Total attributed time is overwhelmingly the gate GEMMs: ~95.6% in the aten::mm cohort, ~1.7% in the prologue, ~0.01% classifier. There is no memory-layout or attention bottleneck; the entire opportunity is (a) engage Tensor Cores via bf16/TF32 (OPT-1), (b) raise GEMM M by hoisting the input projection out of the loop (OPT-2), (c) ideally restore the cuDNN fused RNN path that eliminates the unroll entirely (OPT-3), (d) drop the split-K reduce epilogues and freeze weights (OPT-4).

Recommended application order by pipeline level: (1) OPT-2 at functional level during Dynamo capture (hoist input projection); (2) OPT-3 at functional level is an ALTERNATIVE structural route (cuDNN fused LSTM) that is mutually exclusive with OPT-1/OPT-2 over the recurrent matmuls -- choose the in-place GEMM transforms (OPT-1+OPT-2+OPT-4) for minimal integration risk, or OPT-3 for the largest gain if the model can use nn.LSTM eagerly; (3) OPT-1 at aten level (bf16/TF32 promotion); (4) OPT-4 at inductor_config level (freezing + max_autotune, splitKreduce elimination). Cross-level ordering (functional -> aten -> inductor_config) is enforced by the backend funnel and needs no explicit within-level prerequisite.

duration_ns values are 2-5x inflated by ncu counter-collection application-replay overhead. All estimated_impact.relative_duration_reduction_ns and total_profiled_wall_time_ms values are relative within-profile comparisons, not absolute wall-clock predictions. Impact percentages for OPT-3 vs OPT-1/OPT-2 overlap because they target the same nodes by different mechanisms; do not sum them across the mutually exclusive routes.
